perturbation.original_text: The Company will provide full and free access to the equipment in which to perform service on a 24/7 basis.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will provide access to the equipment in which to perform service during reasonable business hours.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'full and free access on a 24/7 basis' to 'access during reasonable business hours' introduces ambiguity. What constitutes 'reasonable business hours' is undefined, making continuous access uncertain. This could impede the Provider's ability to perform emergency repairs, potentially leading to downtime and service disruptions. The original text provides for 24/7 access, which the modification obfuscates, potentially conflicting with the necessity of providing emergency services around the clock.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text implies a limited access window, which could hinder the 'Services' detailed in Section 2.1(a), specifically 'monitoring, emergency response and emergency splicing.' If the Provider is unable to access the equipment promptly due to the undefined 'reasonable business hours', it would represent a failure to uphold the contract's core purpose, potentially leading to a breach. Delaware contract law dictates that contracts are to be interpreted to give effect to the parties' intentions at the time of contracting; ambiguous terms are typically construed against the drafter.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The original text guarantees 'full and free access' to equipment 24/7. The changed text limits this to 'reasonable business hours,' creating ambiguity and potentially hindering the Provider's ability to fulfill emergency service obligations. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is 'None', Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 'General Obligation and Construction of Contract,' suggesting a focus on contractual obligations and interpretation. The Law Explanation indicates this change may hinder the 'Services', specifically, 'monitoring, emergency response and emergency splicing,' leading to a potential breach. The change introduces ambiguity around access which directly contradicts the original intent of continuous access, potentially violating the contract's core purpose.

perturbation.original_text: The Provider shall undertake commercially reasonable efforts to provide the Services and Repairs. The Provider shall provide the Services and Repairs in a professional and workmanlike manner.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider shall attempt to provide the Services and Repairs. The Provider shall endeavor to provide the Services and Repairs.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'commercially reasonable efforts' with 'attempt to provide' and 'professional and workmanlike manner' with 'endeavor to provide' significantly weakens the Provider's obligation. The original language sets a specific standard of care, while the modified language creates a less stringent obligation and allows for a lower quality of service without necessarily breaching the agreement. This gives the Provider significant leeway in the quality and timeliness of their services.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the language to a less committed stance, the Provider's obligations become significantly more ambiguous. The original obligation to use 'commercially reasonable efforts' to provide services and repairs is replaced with merely 'attempting' to provide them. Under Delaware contract law, there is an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The lowered standards could lead to the provider not fulfilling these expectations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'commercially reasonable efforts' and a 'professional and workmanlike manner,' setting a clear standard. The changed text weakens this to 'attempt to provide' and 'endeavor to provide,' creating ambiguity about the required level of service. The law explanation correctly points out that under Delaware contract law, an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing exists. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that Title 6 of the Delaware Code addresses General Obligation and Construction of Contract. The modified language could allow the provider to not fulfill the implied covenant because of the lowered standards, creating a legal contradiction as the original standard is significantly diminished.

perturbation.original_text: The Provider will obtain and maintain appropriate liability insurance in an amount of not less than $1,000,000 combined single limit for accidents or occurrences which cause bodily injury, death or property damage related to the performance of the Services. The insurance policy willname the Company as an additional insured. The Provider will provide the Company with a certificate of insurance issued to evidence such coverage. Such certificate will provide that there shall be no cancellation, non-renewal, or modification of such coverage without thirty days' prior written notice to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: The Provider will obtain liability insurance. The Provider will name the Company as an additional insured. The Provider will provide the Company with a certificate of insurance issued to evidence such coverage.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific coverage amount of '$1,000,000 combined single limit' and the requirement of 'thirty days' prior written notice to the Company' introduces ambiguity. The term 'liability insurance' is now undefined in scope and coverage level. Removing the 30-day written notice clause means the company could be unaware of policy changes, leaving them exposed to significant and unknown risks. The deletion of specifics about policy adjustments could leave the company vulnerable to unexpected coverage gaps.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing in Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law implies a covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By removing the specificity around the insurance coverage, the Provider could technically comply with the modified clause while obtaining a minimal policy that provides inadequate protection to the Company. The absence of a notification clause upon policy changes hinders the Company's ability to protect its interests and creates asymmetry of information, potentially violating this duty of good faith. The Provider could change insurance providers, lower insurance coverage, or any other similar modification. Since there is no longer a required notice to the Company, the Company will not know of this action until the policy fails to cover an action.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 2 Authenticated PDF Sales Part 1. Short Title, General Construction and Subject Matter Part 2. Form, Formation and Readjustment of Contract Part 3. General Obligation and Construction of Contract Part 4. Title, Creditors and Good Faith Purchases Part 5. Performance Part 6. Breach, Repudiation and Excuse Part 7. Remedies
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the level of liability insurance and eliminates the notification requirement for policy changes, potentially violating the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing as explained in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets do not directly address insurance requirements, the Law Explanation posits that the changes hinder the Company's ability to protect its interests and creates asymmetry of information, suggesting a potential violation of good faith, which Delaware law implies in every contract, per the Law Explanation. The deletion of the specific coverage amount and the removal of the 30-day notice create a substantial risk for the company, rendering the new terms less enforceable and creating an ambiguity that favors the Provider.
